paragraphs.text: The world of Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs) is evolving rapidly, driven by advancements in technology. Over the first quarter of 2026, significant progress has been made, particularly in training platforms, troubleshooting tools, and programming environments. Artificial Intelligence (AI) is a key enabler, accelerating development and making PLC automation more accessible.

While the core electronics and fundamental methodologies of PLC programming remain, the tools available to engineers are transforming. This shift provides greater leverage and efficiency in daily tasks for PLC and system engineers working in manufacturing technology.

The first major trend is the rise of sophisticated training platforms. Previously, learning PLC programming often meant hands-on experience, learning through trial and error. Now, platforms like plc.app and easyplc.school offer structured, AI-assisted training that significantly lowers the barrier to entry.

These resources are often free, a stark contrast to the expensive traditional training programs. They are invaluable for newcomers to automation or IT professionals looking to understand the machinery hardware landscape. Many are open-source, fostering community development.

Troubleshooting PLCs is becoming more efficient with AI-driven tools. Platforms like Snap PLC allow users to upload images of their control cabinets. By analyzing system LEDs and identifying PLC brands and types, these tools can suggest potential root causes and solutions.

While expert knowledge is still crucial for validation, these tools provide valuable guidance, especially for new or unfamiliar systems. They can help engineers resolve issues independently, reducing the need to immediately contact support or colleagues.
The programming landscape for PLCs is also being revolutionized. Several platforms are emerging that leverage AI to streamline development and debugging.

Similar to AI copilots in IT development, PLC Copilot integrates with environments like Codesys. It assists programmers by identifying potential errors and suggesting fixes, drastically reducing debugging time. For example, a task that previously took hours can now be completed in minutes.

Accelera stands out by generating PLC or DCS code directly from functional specifications. This is particularly beneficial for large projects or greenfield implementations where detailed specifications are available. It allows engineers to focus more on the specification itself and less on the intricate details of code generation, also speeding up testing and validation phases.

This approach facilitates clearer communication with stakeholders, including process engineers and end-users, even those without deep PLC programming knowledge. The tool translates specifications into functional code.

Chronax offers an IDE for PLC programming that uses software-oriented languages. This makes PLC development more accessible to individuals with a background in general programming and algorithms, broadening the pool of potential developers beyond traditional PLC engineers. The platform can generate and compile PLC code from these modern languages.

The PLC automation world is experiencing significant advancements in training, troubleshooting, and programming, largely powered by AI. These developments are making the field more accessible and efficient.

We encourage you to stay updated on these trends by subscribing to our newsletter or following our technology reviews. Your insights and comments on these technological shifts are highly valued. Let us know your thoughts in the comments below!
